results.clinical_note: 45 maori woman with 2/12 h/o progressively worsening HA present nocturnally and first thing in the morning with associated blurred vision and nausea

ai_response.verdict_summary: This referral meets P2 criteria for CT Head: progressive headache with nocturnal/morning predominance (positional/raised ICP pattern), associated blurred vision (objective neurological deficit), and nausea — all features of a change in headache pattern with progressive increase in severity plus associated neurological features.
met_criteria: Change in pattern of headaches with progressive increase in frequency or severity [p20]
Associated persistent nausea/vomiting (not typical migraine) [p20]
Objective neurological deficit — blurred vision [p20]
Nocturnal and early-morning predominance consistent with raised ICP pattern (exacerbated by positional/Valsalva-equivalent mechanism) [p20]
add_to_note: Document any history of malignancy (especially lung, melanoma, breast) to support or exclude brain metastases as a differential.
Document any anticoagulant use, history of falls, gait ataxia, or cognitive changes to complete the SOL/bleed/hydrocephalus feature set.
Document any focal neurological signs on examination (e.g. papilloedema on fundoscopy, limb weakness, coordination deficits).
ai_response.suggested_wording: 45-year-old Māori woman with a 2-month history of progressively worsening headache, present nocturnally and on waking in the morning (raised ICP pattern), associated with blurred vision and nausea. No features of typical migraine. Requesting urgent CT Head to exclude space-occupying lesion, bleed, or hydrocephalus.
ai_response.notes: The nocturnal and early-morning timing of headache is a recognised raised intracranial pressure pattern. Blurred vision constitutes an objective neurological deficit. Together with progressive worsening and nausea, this clearly meets the P2 criterion for CT Head. The 'especially aged 50+' qualifier is an epidemiological modifier only and does not exclude this 45-year-old patient. The criteria for cognitive impairment with SOL features (P2 second bullet) were also considered but are not the basis for this decision — the headache-pattern criterion is independently and fully met. No thunderclap or worst-ever headache is described, so SAH emergency pathway is not triggered; however, if the patient reports any sudden-onset severe headache component at any point, immediate ED referral would be warranted.

results.evaluator_verdict: Correct — should proceed
results.evaluator_comment: Correct verdict. Progressive worsening HA with nocturnal/morning pattern, nausea, blurred vision clearly meets criteria.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: P2. Met: 4. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 65yo male w/ unexplained wt loss 5% over past 6/12 with no localising symptoms or signs. Hb mildly low. Ex-smoker.

ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral partially meets P2 criteria for CT CAP but is missing confirmation of strong suspicion of malignancy and at least one additional abnormal blood result to satisfy the male >40 two-pathway criterion, or confirmation that no focal pathology is identified on initial investigations.
met_criteria: Male patient [p10]
Age 65 years (>50 for first pathway; >40 for second pathway) [p10]
Unexplained weight loss 5% over 6 months (meets '>5% over 3–6 months' threshold) [p10]
Low Hb documented ('Hb mildly low' satisfies 'low Hb' as one abnormal blood result) [p10]
No localising symptoms or signs documented (consistent with 'no focal pathology') [p10]
missing_criteria: Strong suspicion of malignancy not explicitly stated — GP must document this clinical judgement [p10]
For the male >50 first pathway: weight loss threshold met and no focal pathology documented, but strong suspicion of malignancy must be explicitly stated [p10]

For the male >40 second pathway: only 1 abnormal blood result documented (low Hb); criteria require 2 or more of: raised CRP, low Hb, raised Ca, high platelets, high ALP, low albumin — persistent on repeat after 3 weeks. A second abnormal blood result is needed, or confirmation that bloods were repeated and remain abnormal [p10]
No documentation that initial investigations have been completed and reviewed prior to CT CAP request [p10]
add_to_note: Add: 'I have a strong clinical suspicion of underlying malignancy based on the combination of unexplained weight loss and low Hb with no localising features.'
Add results of at least one further blood test (CRP, calcium, platelets, ALP, or albumin) to document a second abnormal result if pursuing the male >40 two-abnormal-bloods pathway.
Add: 'Initial investigations including [list tests performed] have been completed and reviewed. No focal pathology or biopsy site identified.'
If using the male >50 no-focal-pathology pathway, confirm: 'No recent USS abdomen/pelvis within 3 months and no CT CAP within 12 months.'
ai_response.suggested_wording: 65-year-old male presenting with unexplained weight loss of 5% over the past 6 months, with no localising symptoms or signs on history or examination. Hb mildly low on bloods. [Add second abnormal blood result, e.g.: 'CRP raised / ALP elevated / platelets high / albumin low — confirmed persistent on repeat bloods after 3 weeks.'] Initial investigations have been completed and reviewed; no focal pathology or biopsy site has been identified. I have a strong clinical suspicion of underlying malignancy. No USS abdomen/pelvis in the past 3 months and no CT CAP in the past 12 months. Requesting CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis.
ai_response.notes: Two P2 pathways are potentially applicable: (1) Male >50 with strong suspicion of malignancy, no focal pathology, and unexplained weight loss >5% over 3–6 months — this pathway does NOT require two abnormal bloods. It requires only that strong suspicion of malignancy is documented and initial investigations have been reviewed. If the GP adds explicit documentation of strong suspicion and confirms initial investigations are complete with no focal pathology, this pathway would be fully met without needing a second abnormal blood result. (2) Male >40 with weight loss >5% over 3–6 months AND 2+ persistently abnormal bloods — currently only one abnormal blood (low Hb) is documented; a second is needed. The simplest path to approval is to document strong suspicion of malignancy and confirm initial investigations are complete, satisfying pathway (1). Ex-smoker status is noted but does not affect CT CAP criteria assessment.

results.evaluator_verdict: At risk — should accept Hb mildly low, still needs 2nd abnormal blood
results.evaluator_comment: "Hb mildly low" should meet "low Hb" criterion without numeric value. Should flag only 1 of 2 required abnormal bloods.
results.notes: Verdict: at_risk. Priority: n/a. Met: 5. Missing: 4.

results.clinical_note: 61yo european male newly diagnosed autoimmune hepatitis – liver edge felt just below RCM. Screen for HCC, cirrhosis

ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral meets P3 criteria for non-pulsatile abdominal mass / suspected enlarged liver on examination in a patient with newly diagnosed autoimmune hepatitis.
met_criteria: Non-pulsatile abdominal mass and suspected enlarged liver on examination — liver edge palpable below right costal margin documented [p31]
ai_response.suggested_wording: 61-year-old European male with newly diagnosed autoimmune hepatitis. On examination, liver edge is palpable just below the right costal margin, consistent with hepatomegaly. Requesting abdominal ultrasound to assess liver size, morphology, and screen for cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
ai_response.notes: The referral meets P3 criteria via the 'non-pulsatile abdominal mass and suspected enlarged liver on examination' pathway [p31]. The HCC surveillance pathway (S3) was also considered but requires initial recommendation from a gastroenterologist or other secondary care specialist, or an agreed local pathway — this gateway is not documented. However, the P3 hepatomegaly pathway is independently and fully met without any gateway requirement, so the verdict is 'proceeds' at P3. Once the ultrasound is complete and the patient is under specialist follow-up, ongoing HCC surveillance should be arranged via the S3 pathway with a gastroenterologist recommendation. Note: screening for AAA and fatty liver is not routinely funded, but the clinical indication here (hepatomegaly with autoimmune hepatitis) is distinct from general screening.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — hepatomegaly pathway independently met
results.evaluator_comment: Should accept on hepatomegaly pathway. HCC surveillance gateway advisory only, not blocking.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: P3. Met: 1. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 64yo f w/ sudden onset R facial numbness + diplopia, lasting 10min. O/E: reduced sensation R face cf L, FROEM without diplopia. Obs OK, pulse regular. ?TIA

ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral meets the Acute 48hr criterion for focal neurological signs, independently of the TIA pathway and its gateway requirements.
met_criteria: Focal neurological signs — reduced sensation right face on examination [p20]
Acute onset episode consistent with focal neurological event [p20]
ai_response.notes: The referral also raises ?TIA. The TIA Acute 48hr pathway requires either: (a) completion of the BPAC TIA decision support tool indicating CT appropriate, (b) recommendation by HNZ neurologist/stroke specialist, or (c) documented inability to access rapid specialist care. None of these gateways are documented. However, the referral independently meets the 'focal neurological signs' Acute 48hr criterion based on the objective finding of reduced right facial sensation on examination — this pathway has no gateway requirement and is fully met. The verdict is therefore 'proceeds' at Acute 48hr on the focal neurological signs criterion alone. Advisory: given the clinical picture of possible TIA (sudden onset, transient, anterior/posterior circulation features including facial numbness and diplopia), consider completing the BPAC TIA decision support tool and documenting the result. If the BPAC tool recommends carotid imaging and the patient is a candidate for carotid endarterectomy, a carotid ultrasound or CTA may also be indicated. Note that diplopia as a symptom may suggest posterior circulation involvement — acute stroke specialist input should be considered. Pulse is documented as regular, which reduces AF concern, but atypical TIA features (diplopia, facial numbness) may warrant specialist assessment.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — focal neurological signs pathway independently met
results.evaluator_comment: ?TIA is a differential. Focal neuro signs independently meet criteria. TIA gateway advisory only.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: Acute 48hr. Met: 2. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 48yo m w/ 8/52 h/o progressive HA – bilateral – sometimes associated nausea – dizziness and vertigo over this period (normal hearing + vision testing). ?SOL

ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral partially meets P2 criteria for CT Head under the progressive headache pathway, but is missing at least one required associated feature from the criteria list.
met_criteria: Change in pattern of headaches with progressive increase in frequency or severity [p20] — 'progressive HA' over 8 weeks satisfies this by clinical shorthand equivalence
Associated with persistent nausea/vomiting (not typical migraine) [p20] — nausea documented
Patient aged 48 — epidemiological modifier 'especially aged 50+' does not exclude this patient; it is a soft qualifier only [p20]
missing_criteria: The P2 progressive headache criterion requires the change in headache pattern to be associated with AT LEAST ONE of: (a) chronic illness with potential cerebral complications especially malignancy, OR (b) exacerbated by coughing/sneezing/Valsalva, OR (c) associated with persistent nausea/vomiting (not typical migraine), OR (d) objective neurological deficit [p20]. Nausea is documented and appears to satisfy (c), BUT the note states nausea is only 'sometimes associated' — it is ambiguous whether this meets 'persistent nausea'. Clarify whether nausea is persistent or intermittent, and confirm it is not typical of migraine pattern.
add_to_note: Clarify whether the nausea is persistent (present most of the time) rather than intermittent, and confirm this is not a typical migraine pattern — this is required to satisfy the P2 associated feature criterion.

Document whether any objective neurological deficit is present on examination (e.g. cranial nerve signs, motor/sensory deficit, coordination abnormality) — if present, this independently satisfies the associated feature requirement.
Document whether the headache is exacerbated by coughing, sneezing, or Valsalva manoeuvre.
Document whether the patient has any chronic illness with potential cerebral complications (e.g. known malignancy, immunosuppression, HIV).
Document whether the patient is on anticoagulant therapy (relevant to cognitive/SOL sub-criteria).
ai_response.suggested_wording: 48-year-old male with an 8-week history of progressive bilateral headache with increasing frequency and severity. Associated with persistent nausea (not a typical migraine pattern). Also reports dizziness and vertigo. Hearing and vision testing normal. No objective neurological deficit identified on examination [or: objective neurological deficit present — specify]. Headache [is / is not] exacerbated by coughing, sneezing, or Valsalva. No known malignancy or chronic illness with cerebral complications. Query space-occupying lesion. Requesting P2 CT Head.
ai_response.notes: The ?SOL query raises the cognitive impairment with SOL features pathway [p20], but no cognitive impairment is documented — this pathway cannot be assessed. The progressive headache pathway [p20] is the most applicable. The key ambiguity is whether 'sometimes associated nausea' constitutes 'persistent nausea/vomiting' as required by the criterion. If the GP can confirm nausea is persistent and not a typical migraine pattern, or documents any objective neurological deficit, the referral would fully meet P2 criteria and proceed. Dizziness and vertigo are noted but do not independently satisfy any listed associated feature under this criterion. The epidemiological modifier 'especially aged 50+' does not exclude this 48-year-old patient — it is a soft qualifier only and must not be used to decline or downgrade the referral.

results.evaluator_verdict: At risk — "progressive" meets pattern change; still missing one associated feature
results.evaluator_comment: "Progressive" should satisfy change in pattern element. Still needs Valsalva/persistent nausea/neuro/malignancy.
results.notes: Verdict: at_risk. Priority: P2. Met: 3. Missing: 1.

results.clinical_note: 8yo boy 5/12 h/o progressive pain R lower leg – ?knee ?ankle. Pain during the day and night. Limp at times. Stopping him from doing normal activities (basketball and karate). R/O overt malignancy.

ai_response.verdict_summary: An 8-year-old boy with 5 months of progressive right lower leg pain including night pain and limp meets P2 criteria for urgent X-ray under both the Knee and Feet/Ankle pathways.
met_criteria: Undiagnosed persistent knee/lower leg pain for one week or more (5 months documented) [p104]
Pain at rest and night-waking with pain explicitly documented [p104]
Undiagnosed persistent bone or joint pain for one week or more with pain at rest or night-waking with pain (ankle/foot pathway) [p103]
Functional impairment documented (limp, unable to participate in basketball and karate) [p104, p103]
add_to_note: Consider adding whether there is any bony swelling, joint deformity, or localised tenderness on examination to assist radiologist targeting.
Note: guidance for knee pain in children requires exclusion of hip pathology — if no knee pathology is found on examination, arrange AP pelvis and frog lateral hip X-ray as well [p104].
ai_response.suggested_wording: 8-year-old boy with a 5-month history of progressive pain in the right lower leg, affecting the knee and ankle regions. Pain is present during the day and at night (night-waking documented). He has a limp at times and is unable to participate in his normal activities (basketball and karate). Query underlying bony pathology — rule out malignancy. Requesting urgent X-ray of right knee and right ankle/lower leg.
ai_response.notes: The note queries both knee and ankle involvement. Both the Knee X-Ray [p104] and Feet X-Ray [p103] P2 criteria are independently met on the basis of persistent pain >1 week with documented night pain and functional impairment. The concern for malignancy ('R/O overt malignancy') is clinically appropriate given the duration, night pain, and progressive nature — the radiologist should be made aware. Per knee X-ray guidance [p104], if no knee pathology is identified on examination, hip pathology must be excluded with AP pelvis and frog lateral views. A P2 (within 2 weeks) priority applies. If X-ray is unremarkable but symptoms persist, further imaging (MRI) via secondary care referral may be warranted.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — paediatric bone pain criteria met
results.evaluator_comment: Progressive pain, night pain, functional impairment in child. Should proceed on paediatric pathway.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: P2. Met: 4. Missing: 0. [paed]

results.clinical_note: 75yo m w/ new AKI, eGFR 3 (normally 55). No clear cause.

ai_response.verdict_summary: Acute renal function deterioration with a dramatic eGFR drop from 55 to 3 meets the Acute 48hr criterion for urgent renal ultrasound.
met_criteria: Acute renal function deterioration — serum creatinine increase consistent with severe acute-on-chronic kidney injury (eGFR drop from baseline 55 to 3, representing profound acute deterioration) [p53]
No clear cause identified, supporting need for imaging [p53]
add_to_note: Document the actual serum creatinine values (current and recent baseline) to formally confirm the acute deterioration threshold (increase >50 micromol/L in 7 days or >25 micromol/L in 2 days).
Document the timeframe over which the eGFR drop occurred (days) to confirm acute vs subacute onset.

Note: given eGFR of 3, discussion with secondary care or PCRL is strongly recommended as per criteria guidance — this patient may require acute nephrology assessment rather than community imaging alone.
ai_response.suggested_wording: 75-year-old male presenting with new acute kidney injury. Baseline eGFR approximately 55, now critically reduced to 3 — representing a severe acute-on-chronic deterioration with no clear cause identified. Creatinine [insert current value] vs recent baseline [insert value], change occurring over [insert timeframe in days]. Requesting urgent renal ultrasound within 48 hours. Secondary care discussion recommended given severity.
ai_response.safety_alert: eGFR of 3 represents critical renal failure. While the Acute 48hr ultrasound criterion is met, this patient should be discussed urgently with secondary care (nephrology) — community imaging alone may be insufficient and acute admission may be required.
ai_response.notes: The CT KUB red-flag criterion (eGFR <45 → admit, do not image) applies to CT KUB only and does not block renal ultrasound referral. The renal ultrasound Acute 48hr criterion for acute renal function deterioration is independently met. However, the clinical severity (eGFR 3) warrants a parallel urgent secondary care discussion. The creatinine values and exact timeframe are not explicitly stated — the add_to_note items request these to strengthen documentation, but the eGFR drop from 55 to 3 is sufficient on its face to satisfy the qualitative threshold of acute renal function deterioration.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — surface P1 AKI and P2, note conflicting dispositions
results.evaluator_comment: Should surface all matching pathways. eGFR 3 is severe AKI — should flag P1 Acute 48hr.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: Acute 48hr. Met: 2. Missing: 0.

ai_response.notes: Step 0 redirect applies: the primary cause of back pain is a documented trauma mechanism (stepping down from a ladder). CRR criteria assessment is not applicable. The GP should lodge an ACC claim and request spinal imaging through ACC. Note also that even absent the ACC redirect, acute uncomplicated spine pain without red flags is not routinely funded under CRR within the first 6 weeks — however at 3 months duration some CRR criteria could potentially apply if the ACC redirect did not exist. The ACC redirect takes precedence regardless.

results.evaluator_verdict: Redirect to ACC — trauma mechanism

Symptom acuity and onset not documented — 'sudden onset severe pelvic pain' would trigger Acute 24hr suspected ovarian cyst accident pathway; gradual onset would direct to a different priority [p46]

Document whether the IUD was found to be broken or only partially removed on any prior attempt.

Document duration of symptoms.

ai_response.suggested_wording: 35-year-old female with Mirena IUD. Presenting with [duration] of lower abdominal cramping pain and PV bleeding. IUD strings are [visible and in normal position / not palpable / missing] on examination. Patient is clinically stable. Query IUD malposition or perforation. If the patient is clinically unstable, she should be referred for acute assessment without imaging. The note as written does not explicitly document string status, which is the crux of the IUD malposition/perforation pathway.

This is directly applicable — knee examination is documented as normal, and hip pathology (restricted ROM, mild pain, limp) is present. The P2 criterion 'hip or knee pain in child with limp, suspected Perthes disease' is met. SUFE is also a differential in a 13-year-old; if the child is overweight or unable to weight-bear, the acute SUFE redirect to ED applies instead. The note does not document weight/BMI, so this must be checked before proceeding with community imaging. Knee X-ray is not indicated given normal knee examination — hip imaging is the correct pathway.

Confirm microscopic haematuria is persistent (document how many episodes or over what time period).
Confirm UTI has been excluded on urine microscopy/culture (already implied but state explicitly, e.g., 'MSU microscopy and culture negative').
ai_response.suggested_wording: 64-year-old female patient with persisting microscopic haematuria. UTI excluded on urine microscopy (no infection identified). Non-smoker. No weight loss or other symptoms. Works as a hairdresser. Requesting renal ultrasound for investigation of microscopic haematuria in a female patient of any age per CRR criteria [p53].
ai_response.notes: The applicable criterion is 'Microscopic haematuria — female, of any age' at P4 [p53]. This criterion applies regardless of smoking status or age in female patients. The male-specific P3 criterion ('male patient, older than 50 years, current or ex-smoker') is gender-inapplicable here and has not been assessed. Note that macroscopic haematuria in a female aged 40–85 would qualify at P2 via CT IVU [p24], but this referral documents microscopic haematuria only. The word 'lady' strongly implies female sex; however, under the strict documentation standard, explicit statement is preferred. The referral is very likely to proceed once the note is clarified with explicit sex documentation and confirmation of persistent haematuria.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — persistent microscopic haematuria, no infection
results.evaluator_comment: Persistent microscopic haematuria, infection excluded. Should proceed.
results.notes: Verdict: at_risk. Priority: P4. Met: 4. Missing: 1.

results.clinical_note: 62yo male w/ unexplained weight loss 8% over 4 months. No focal pathology. CRP raised, Hb low, albumin low. Ex-smoker.

ai_response.verdict_summary: This referral meets P2 criteria for CT CAP: male over 40 with strong suspicion of malignancy, no focal pathology, weight loss >5% over 3–6 months, and 3 of the required abnormal blood markers (raised CRP, low Hb, low albumin) persisting.
met_criteria: Male >40 years (62yo male) [p10]
Strong suspicion of malignancy with no focal pathology identified [p10]
Unintentional unexplained weight loss >5% (8% documented) [p10]
Weight loss occurred over 3–6 months (4 months) [p10]
2 or more persistently abnormal bloods: raised CRP ✓, low Hb ✓, low albumin ✓ (3 of 6 qualifying markers present) [p10]
add_to_note: Confirm that blood results are persistent on repeat testing after 3 weeks, as required by criteria — document this explicitly in the referral note.
Confirm no recent ultrasound abdomen/pelvis within last 3 months and no CT CAP within last 12 months, as these would exclude CRR funding.
Confirm patient is fit for treatment and willing to undergo further investigation.
ai_response.suggested_wording: 62-year-old male, ex-smoker, presenting with unexplained unintentional weight loss of 8% over 4 months. No focal pathology identified on examination or initial investigations. Strong clinical suspicion of underlying malignancy. Blood results show raised CRP, low Hb, and low albumin — all confirmed as persistently abnormal on repeat testing after 3 weeks. No recent ultrasound abdomen/pelvis within 3 months and no CT CAP within 12 months. Patient is fit for and willing to undergo further investigation. Requesting CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis to investigate for underlying malignancy.
ai_response.notes: The criteria for the male >40 pathway require that the abnormal bloods are 'persistent on repeat after 3 weeks' — this is not explicitly stated in the referral note. The add_to_note items flag this for documentation. If repeat testing has not yet been done, the referral may be at risk. The male >50 pathway (no repeat-testing requirement for bloods) is also met on age and weight loss alone, providing an additional basis for acceptance. Three abnormal markers are present (CRP, Hb, albumin), all of which are on the qualifying list (raised CRP, low Hb, low albumin). The out-of-criteria exclusions (current cancer under follow-up, investigated by secondary care within 12 months for same symptoms, recent USS abdomen/pelvis within 3 months, CT CAP within 12 months) should be confirmed as absent.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — all CT CAP criteria met (3 abnormal bloods, weight loss ≥5% over ≥3 months)
results.evaluator_comment: 4 months meets 3-6 month window. 3 abnormal bloods documented. Should proceed.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: P2. Met: 5. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 58yo m w/ sudden onset L arm weakness and facial droop, resolved after 20 minutes. O/E: mild residual L facial weakness. BP 165/95. ?TIA

ai_response.verdict_summary: This referral meets the Acute 48hr CT Head criterion for focal neurological signs, independently of the TIA pathway gateway requirements.
met_criteria: Focal neurological signs — residual left facial weakness on examination documented [p20]

?TIA pathway also identified: episode within 7 days, patient aged 58 years (under 59, high-risk feature per ABCD2/age criterion), BP 165/95 suggesting vascular risk, anterior circulation symptoms (facial droop, arm weakness) [p20]
add_to_note: Document the exact date and time of the episode to confirm it is within 7 days (required for TIA pathway).

Document whether the BPAC TIA decision support tool has been completed, or whether a HNZ neurologist/stroke specialist has recommended CT — this is required to formally invoke the TIA pathway (though the focal neurological signs pathway is already met independently).
Document whether the patient is in atrial fibrillation or on anticoagulants — if either applies, redirect to acute on-call stroke specialist assessment rather than community CT.
If anterior circulation TIA is confirmed and patient is a candidate for carotid endarterectomy, consider adding CTA carotid at the same visit per background guidance [p20, p36].
ai_response.suggested_wording: 58-year-old male presenting with sudden onset left arm weakness and facial droop, fully resolved within 20 minutes. On examination: mild residual left facial weakness (focal neurological sign). BP 165/95. Not in atrial fibrillation. Not on anticoagulants. Episode occurred within the last 7 days. Query TIA — anterior circulation symptoms. BPAC TIA tool completed [or: patient unable to access rapid specialist care]. Requesting Acute 48hr CT Head +/- CTA carotid given focal neurological signs and suspected anterior circulation TIA.
ai_response.notes: The referral independently meets the Acute 48hr criterion for 'focal neurological signs' (residual left facial weakness on examination) — this pathway has no gateway requirement and is fully met as documented. The TIA-specific pathway (also Acute 48hr) requires either BPAC tool completion, HNZ neurologist/stroke specialist recommendation, or documented inability to access rapid specialist care — this gateway is not documented, so the TIA pathway is not independently met; however, this does not affect the verdict since the focal neurological signs pathway is fully met. Advisory: if the patient is found to be in AF or on anticoagulants, this becomes a high-risk TIA and should be redirected to acute on-call stroke specialist assessment rather than community imaging [p36]. Background guidance notes that for suspected anterior circulation TIA where the patient is a candidate for carotid endarterectomy, CTA carotid (or carotid ultrasound if CTA unavailable) should be added to the CT Head request [p20].

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — focal neuro signs independently met; TIA gateway advisory
results.evaluator_comment: Residual focal weakness documented. Should proceed on focal neuro pathway.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: Acute 48hr. Met: 2. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 55yo male, known Hep C. Liver palpable 3cm below costal margin, firm and non-tender. Referred for HCC surveillance and assessment of hepatomegaly.

ai_response.verdict_title: Referral Proceeds — P3 (Non-palpable abdominal mass / hepatomegaly on examination)
ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral meets P3 criteria for ultrasound abdomen on the basis of a non-pulsatile abdominal mass and suspected enlarged liver on examination, independently of the HCC surveillance pathway.
met_criteria: Non-pulsatile abdominal mass and suspected enlarged liver on examination — liver palpable 3cm below costal margin, firm and non-tender [p31]
ai_response.suggested_wording: 55-year-old male with known Hepatitis C. Liver palpable 3cm below costal margin, firm and non-tender on examination — hepatomegaly confirmed clinically. Requesting ultrasound abdomen for assessment of hepatomegaly (non-pulsatile abdominal mass on examination).
ai_response.notes: Two pathways were identified: (1) P3 — Non-pulsatile abdominal mass / suspected enlarged liver on examination [p31]: FULLY MET — liver palpable 3cm below costal margin is explicit clinical hepatomegaly. This pathway is the basis for the proceeds verdict. (2) S3 — HCC surveillance as initially recommended by a gastroenterologist or other secondary care specialist [p31]: NOT MET — the criteria require that HCC surveillance ultrasound be initiated on the recommendation of a gastroenterologist or other secondary care specialist, or per an agreed local pathway. No such specialist recommendation is documented in this note. The GP should seek gastroenterology input to establish a formal HCC surveillance programme for this patient with Hepatitis C, which would then support ongoing S3 surveillance referrals. The unmet HCC surveillance gateway does not affect the proceeds verdict, which is secured by the independently met hepatomegaly criterion.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — hepatomegaly pathway independently met
results.evaluator_comment: Hepatomegaly documented (3cm below costal margin). Should proceed on hepatomegaly pathway.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: P3. Met: 1. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 68yo f w/ acute kidney injury, eGFR dropped from 60 to 12 over 5 days. No obstruction suspected. No clear cause identified.

ai_response.verdict_summary: Acute renal function deterioration with eGFR drop meeting threshold criteria; referral proceeds at Acute 48hr priority, with strong recommendation to discuss with secondary care or PCRL given severity.
met_criteria: Acute renal function deterioration — serum creatinine equivalent: eGFR dropped from 60 to 12 over 5 days, consistent with a creatinine rise well exceeding >50 micromol/L in 7 days [p53]
add_to_note: Document the actual serum creatinine values (baseline and current) to explicitly confirm the >50 micromol/L rise in 7 days threshold, as criteria reference creatinine rather than eGFR.
Note that discussion with secondary care or PCRL is recommended per criteria guidance for this presentation.
ai_response.suggested_wording: 68-year-old female presenting with acute kidney injury. eGFR has dropped from 60 to 12 over 5 days (serum creatinine rise consistent with >50 micromol/L increase within 7 days). No obstruction suspected clinically. No clear cause identified. Requesting urgent renal ultrasound. Secondary care/PCRL discussion planned.
ai_response.safety_alert: This degree of acute kidney injury (eGFR 12) is clinically severe. Ensure secondary care discussion is occurring in parallel with or instead of community imaging — hospital admission may be required. This tool confirms criteria are met but does not assess whether community imaging is clinically safe given the severity.
ai_response.notes: The CT KUB red flag threshold (eGFR <45) is not applicable here as this is a renal ultrasound referral, not CT KUB. The eGFR drop from 60 to 12 over 5 days satisfies the acute renal function deterioration criterion at p53 (creatinine increase >50 micromol/L in 7 days is implied by this magnitude of eGFR change, though explicit creatinine values should be added to the note). The criteria guidance explicitly recommends discussion with secondary care or PCRL for this presentation — this is advisory and does not prevent the referral from proceeding.

results.evaluator_verdict: Proceeds — surface P1 and P2, note conflicting dispositions
results.evaluator_comment: Severe AKI — should surface P1 Acute 48hr. Note conflicting dispositions.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: Acute 48hr. Met: 1. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 45yo m, episode of slurred speech and R hand clumsiness lasting 30 minutes yesterday. Fully resolved. BP normal. ?TIA. No other focal signs currently.

ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral meets the Acute 48hr criterion for focal neurological signs independently of the TIA pathway, so it proceeds without requiring BPAC tool completion.
met_criteria: Focal neurological signs — slurred speech (dysarthria) and right hand clumsiness documented [p20]
Episode within last 7 days (yesterday) [p20]
ai_response.suggested_wording: 45-year-old male presenting with an episode of slurred speech and right hand clumsiness lasting 30 minutes yesterday, now fully resolved. BP normal. Focal neurological signs documented. Query TIA. Requesting Acute CT Head within 48 hours.
ai_response.notes: The referral independently meets the Acute 48hr 'focal neurological signs' criterion [p20] based on the documented transient dysarthria and right hand clumsiness. This pathway does not require BPAC TIA tool completion. The ?TIA pathway is also potentially applicable (episode within 7 days), but that pathway requires either BPAC tool completion, HNZ neurologist/stroke specialist recommendation, or documented inability to access rapid specialist care — none of which are documented. Because the focal neurological signs pathway is fully met, the verdict is 'proceeds' regardless of the TIA gateway status. Advisory: given the clinical picture of a likely anterior circulation TIA in a 45-year-old, consider also whether CTA carotid should be added per the CT Head background guidance (candidate for carotid endarterectomy assessment). If the patient is a candidate for carotid endarterectomy, carotid angiography (CTA) or carotid ultrasound should be considered alongside the CT Head. Urgent stroke/TIA specialist review is also strongly recommended given the high-risk features (age <59, significant deficit duration, anterior circulation symptoms).

results.evaluator_verdict: At risk — focal neuro signs transiently met; TIA gateway advisory; note history of focal signs
results.evaluator_comment: Transient focal signs still relevant. Should be at_risk with note about TIA gateway.
results.notes: Verdict: proceeds. Priority: Acute 48hr. Met: 2. Missing: 0.

results.clinical_note: 70yo f w/ unexplained weight loss 3% over 2 months. Low Hb, raised CRP.

ai_response.verdict_title: At Risk — Key Criteria Not Met
ai_response.verdict_summary: The referral does not yet meet CT CAP criteria: weight loss is only 3% (threshold is >5%) over 2 months (threshold is 3–6 months), and only 2 abnormal bloods are present but the age/gender-specific pathway requiring them also requires weight loss >5% over 3–6 months.
met_criteria: Female aged >60 years [p10]
Low Hb documented [p10]
Raised CRP documented [p10]
No focal pathology implied (no localising features mentioned) [p10]
missing_criteria: Weight loss threshold not met: only 3% documented — criteria require >5% [p10]
Duration threshold not met: 2 months documented — criteria require over 3–6 months [p10]
Strong suspicion of malignancy not explicitly stated [p10]
Only 2 abnormal bloods documented (Hb, CRP) — the female >50 pathway requires weight loss >5% over 3–6 months AND 2+ of the listed bloods; weight loss threshold must also be met [p10]
The simpler female >60 pathway (unintentional unexplained weight loss >5% over 3–6 months) also requires weight loss >5% over 3–6 months [p10]
add_to_note: Document the total percentage of weight loss — criteria require >5% unintentional unexplained weight loss.
Document the duration of weight loss — criteria require this to have occurred over a 3–6 month period.
Explicitly state strong clinical suspicion of underlying malignancy with no focal pathology identified.

Document whether additional abnormal bloods are present (raised Ca, high platelets, high ALP, low albumin) — 2 or more from the specified list alongside weight loss >5% over 3–6 months would support the P2 pathway.
Confirm no recent USS abdomen/pelvis within 3 months and no CT CAP within 12 months (out-of-criteria exclusions).
ai_response.suggested_wording: 70-year-old female presenting with unintentional unexplained weight loss of [X]% over [X] months, associated with [abdominal pain / fatigue / nausea — include if present]. I have a strong clinical suspicion of underlying malignancy with no focal pathology or biopsy site identified on examination or initial investigations. Blood results show persistently low Hb and raised CRP [include any additional abnormal results: raised Ca, high platelets, high ALP, low albumin if applicable], confirmed on repeat testing. No recent USS abdomen/pelvis within 3 months and no CT CAP within 12 months. Requesting CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis.
ai_response.notes: Both P2 CT CAP pathways for this patient's age and gender require weight loss >5% over 3–6 months as a hard numeric threshold. The documented 3% over 2 months does not meet either threshold. If the GP's clinical assessment supports a stronger suspicion of malignancy and the weight loss figure or duration is greater than documented, the note should be updated accordingly. If criteria genuinely cannot be met, discussion with a PCRL or secondary care specialist is recommended as an alternative route to imaging per the CT CAP guidance.

results.evaluator_verdict: At risk or declined — weight loss 3% (below 5% threshold) AND 2 months (below 3-month threshold)
results.evaluator_comment: Both weight loss % and duration fall below thresholds. Should be declined or at_risk.
results.notes: Verdict: at_risk. Priority: n/a. Met: 4. Missing: 5.
